picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA delivered a blockbuster Q1 FY2027 with revenue of $81.6B (+85.2% YoY), EPS of $2.39 (+214.5% YoY) versus an estimate of $1.79 — a 33%+ bottom-line beat — while Data Center revenue grew 69% YoY and now constitutes ~90% of total revenues. The AI capex supercycle remains the dominant earnings revision engine in the market, with hyperscalers committing ~$800B in capex in 2026. Today's intra-day pullback to ~$207 (off the $236 52-week high) creates a better risk/reward entry — the thesis is intact but the stock has traded sideways for three months, absorbing the fundamental beats at a healthier valuation.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings (expected late August) with Blackwell Ultra ramp cadence and forward Data Center revenue guidance reset
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$207, down ~12% from 52-week high of $236.54; P/S of ~21.5x is elevated vs. sector median but justified by 85%+ revenue growth; consolidating above the $199 support established today — constructive base-building pattern
picks.exitTrigger: Data Center revenue growth decelerates below 40% YoY, or a major hyperscaler publicly announces meaningful NVDA chip substitution with custom silicon at scale
picks.keyRisk: Hyperscaler customer concentration (~90% of revenue in Data Center) means any capex pullback or pivot to custom ASICs creates outsized revenue downside

picks.name: Alphabet Inc. Class A
picks.rationale: Alphabet is one of the least extended Mag-7 names at ~20x forward P/E, with Cloud now exceeding $12B/quarter run rate, AI Overviews monetizing in Search, and a first-ever dividend signaling FCF confidence. Berkshire Hathaway nearly tripled its Alphabet stake in Q1 2026 — purchasing ~40M additional shares to bring total to ~57.8M shares worth ~$16.6B — making it the most high-conviction smart-money endorsement in the entire Q1 13F cycle. Today's dip to $363 (vs. 52-week high of $408.61) extends the entry advantage further.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Cloud growth acceleration above $13B/quarter and AI Overviews monetization disclosure
picks.entryNote: Trading at $363, approximately 11% below 52-week high of $408.61; ~20x forward P/E is a meaningful discount to SPX and to NVDA/META — one of the best value-growth combinations in large-cap tech
picks.exitTrigger: Cloud growth decelerates below 25% YoY, or DOJ antitrust ruling mandates structural remedy breaking up Search distribution
picks.keyRisk: DOJ search antitrust remedy (structural breakup vs. behavioral remedy) remains an overhang — a forced Chrome/Android divestiture would be a material negative surprise

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom is the premier custom AI silicon (XPU) and networking infrastructure play — the direct beneficiary of hyperscalers designing their own AI chips rather than buying off-the-shelf NVDA GPUs. The sector rotation scorecard confirms semis and AI infrastructure as Tier 1 leaders with over $12B in Q2 tech ETF inflows, and Druckenmiller specifically added AVGO as a new position in Q1 2026. Recent headwinds from a 'disappointing outlook' and investor caution on Fed rate hikes represent a buying opportunity in a structurally superior franchise.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings (September) — custom AI ASIC revenue from Google TPU and Meta MTIA design wins disclosed with updated multi-year roadmap
picks.entryNote: Facing near-term headwinds on guidance; use pullback as accumulation opportunity — custom AI silicon TAM expansion from hyperscaler insourcing is a 3-5 year structural re-rating story not yet fully priced in
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of Google or Meta custom ASIC design wins, or VMware integration generates material customer churn above 15% of acquired ARR
picks.keyRisk: VMware integration execution risk plus customer concentration in AI chip design wins — losing one major hyperscaler partner would trigger significant estimate cuts

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Tepper nearly doubled his Amazon stake in Q1 2026 (+98%, adding 2.1M shares to 4.3M total, making it ~15.2% of Appaloosa's portfolio) — explicitly betting on AI infrastructure investments converting into AWS margin expansion. Ackman's Pershing Square also moved Amazon to its second-largest position. AWS is the highest-margin, fastest-growing segment and benefits directly from the AI capex supercycle as cloud inference workloads scale. Amazon's e-commerce remains defensive in a nominal-GDP-growth environment, providing an earnings floor beneath the AI optionality.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/August) — AWS margin expansion disclosure and Trainium AI chip volume ramp update
picks.entryNote: AWS growth re-accelerating from 17% to 20%+ quarterly; stock has partially recovered from its ~20% drop on capex concerns — entry here benefits from Tepper and Ackman accumulation at similar or higher prices
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerates below 15% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or capex guidance exceeds $100B without a clear operating leverage path
picks.keyRisk: Aggressive capex ($85B+ guided for 2026) with still-unclear near-term ROI creates valuation overhang if AWS growth disappoints

picks.name: Oracle Corporation
picks.rationale: Oracle is the most underappreciated AI infrastructure beneficiary: its Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) is the preferred multi-cloud AI training platform for enterprises due to superior GPU cluster density and favorable pricing versus AWS/Azure. The momentum screen ranked it in the top 10 universe, and it has appeared in the portfolio since June 3 at high conviction, reflecting consistent institutional accumulation. Earnings revisions have been trending sharply higher alongside cloud revenue reacceleration, and the stock is trading at +2.86% today per market data — notably outperforming the broader tech selloff.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings (mid-June 2026) — cloud RPO backlog and AI infrastructure bookings update expected to drive estimate revisions sharply higher
picks.entryNote: Stock up ~2.86% today while broad tech sold off — relative strength signal in a down tape; cloud revenue tracking toward 25%+ growth with RPO backlog providing revenue visibility 12-18 months forward
picks.exitTrigger: OCI bookings growth decelerates below 20% YoY, or hyperscaler customers consolidate AI workloads away from multi-cloud toward single-vendor platforms
picks.keyRisk: Competitive displacement risk from AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud on AI training workloads as they build out proprietary GPU cluster capacity

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman delivered Q1 2026 EPS of $17.55 (+24% YoY) with a 6.56% beat, record Global Banking & Markets revenues of $12.7B, and 19.8% annualized ROE — all pointing to sustained earnings power. The sector rotation scorecard flags financials as 'coiling' toward leadership on yield curve steepening and M&A/IPO pipeline revival, while the macro briefing confirms the easiest regulatory regime in the post-GFC era for capital markets activity. At below 12x forward P/E, GS is the highest-quality financial franchise available at a meaningful discount to the broader market's 25x trailing multiple.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) — M&A advisory fee surge from pipeline built during Q1 deal activity, plus trading revenue beat driven by elevated macro volatility (VIX ~20)
picks.entryNote: Trading below 12x forward earnings — far from extended; 52-week high recently tested with positive price momentum; capital markets reactivation cycle typically lasts 6-8 quarters once started
picks.exitTrigger: ROE falls below 15% on credit quality deterioration, or M&A advisory revenue declines 25%+ on deal freeze from Fed hike shock
picks.keyRisk: A forced Fed rate hike scenario (CPI >4.5%) could freeze M&A activity and widen credit spreads, compressing capital markets revenues sharply

picks.name: RTX Corporation (Raytheon Technologies)
picks.rationale: RTX is the premier defense and aerospace compounder — a structural beneficiary of elevated global defense budgets driven by the Iran war and NATO commitments, while its Pratt & Whitney engine division is riding a multi-year commercial aerospace aftermarket upcycle. The sector rotation scorecard ranks Industrials as Tier 1, with defense/electrification as the highest-conviction sub-sectors. RTX has appeared in every prior portfolio since May 29 at high conviction with a thesis that has not broken. Q1 2026 backlog growth and upward earnings revisions are tracking positively across the defense complex.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — defense segment bookings update with potential Stinger/Patriot system restocking orders tied to geopolitical escalation
picks.entryNote: Industrials up 16%+ YTD with defense names leading; RTX at a modest premium to 10-year median P/E (~20x) justified by structurally higher backlog and dual defense/commercial cycle support
picks.exitTrigger: Iran ceasefire becomes permanent and defense budgets are revised down, or Pratt & Whitney GTF engine grounding materially expands beyond current scope
picks.keyRisk: A surprise full Iran ceasefire + Hormuz reopening would reduce geopolitical risk premium and rotate capital away from defense names — though commercial aero would partially offset

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: Energy is the only sector trading at a discount both to the overall S&P 500 AND to its own 10-year average P/E — roughly 32% cheap on each measure — while energy stocks are up 22%+ YTD as the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed. XOM is the highest-quality integrated oil name: Morgan Stanley's base case has crude returning to ~$90/barrel by year-end, XOM has the broadest positive earnings revision breadth of any company in the S&P 500, and it was a top holding for Berkshire (Chevron at 6.64% of portfolio) with energy representing a structurally undervalued inflation hedge. Energy adds critical sector diversification vs. the tech-heavy growth book.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Permian production volume beat plus Pioneer integration synergy update with updated $90/bbl price deck guidance
picks.entryNote: Energy up 22%+ YTD but still cheapest sector on combined absolute/relative 10-year P/E; XOM trades at ~13x forward earnings vs. market at ~21x — 40% valuation discount with positive momentum
picks.exitTrigger: WTI crude falls sustainably below $65/barrel on coordinated OPEC+ supply increase AND Hormuz fully reopened; or Pioneer integration costs materially exceed synergy targets
picks.keyRisk: A surprise US-Iran peace deal and full Hormuz reopening could spike oil supply and compress energy earnings by 20-30% within 2-3 quarters

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: S&P Global is a capital-light, high-ROIC compounder with three structural growth drivers: (1) debt issuance volumes surging as corporates refinance ahead of any potential Fed rate hike, (2) Market Intelligence and Commodity Insights divisions benefiting from the energy supercycle and AI data demand, and (3) Platts commodity pricing data seeing elevated usage amid Iran war supply disruption. SPGI has appeared consistently in the portfolio since May 29 with stable high conviction. At ~28x forward P/E, it is not cheap, but the earnings quality — recurring subscription revenues, pricing power, regulatory moat — justifies the premium in a late-cycle environment.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — ratings issuance volume update reflecting Q2 corporate debt calendar, with upside from Commodity Insights pricing data demand
picks.entryNote: Trading near all-time highs on sustained earnings power; ~28x forward P/E is a slight premium but below 5-year average of 32x given compressed multiples across the sector; recurring revenue base de-risks the multiple
picks.exitTrigger: Debt issuance volumes drop 30%+ from Fed-induced credit freeze, or regulatory action threatens ratings agency oligopoly economics
picks.keyRisk: A forced Fed rate hike cycle that freezes investment-grade issuance would directly compress ratings revenue — the most rate-sensitive revenue line in the business

picks.name: Merck & Co. Inc.
picks.rationale: Merck is the highest-quality defensive growth name in the Health Care sector — Keytruda (pembrolizumab) remains the world's best-selling oncology drug and its pipeline has multiple Phase 3 readouts scheduled through 2027 that could extend the franchise beyond the 2028 patent cliff. XLV trades at 19.94x forward P/E below its own 10-year median, and healthcare M&A has reached $106B in 2026 per PitchBook, creating potential acqui-hire upside. MRK has appeared consistently in the portfolio since May 29 as a quality anchor, providing sector diversification and inflation-resistant earnings in a stagflationary regime.
picks.catalyst: Phase 3 readout for MK-1654 (mRNA RSV vaccine) and subcutaneous Keytruda approval decision expected H2 2026 — extending Keytruda franchise duration is the key de-risking event
picks.entryNote: MRK at ~17-18x forward P/E — trading at a discount to the already-discounted XLV sector; 3.2% dividend yield provides income support; stock has lagged broad market YTD, creating mean-reversion potential
picks.exitTrigger: Keytruda patent cliff accelerates via surprise BIOSIMILAR approval, or Medicare drug pricing negotiation forces >20% price reduction on Keytruda ahead of 2028 schedule
picks.keyRisk: 2028 Keytruda patent cliff is the existential overhang — if subcutaneous formulation and IV-to-SC conversion fail to protect market share, revenue could decline 30%+ post-cliff

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares
picks.rationale: Gold at $4,521/oz with GLD near $397 is in an intermediate correction (down ~14.8% over 3 months from $509 52-week high) — but the structural bid remains intact. GLD has outperformed SPY +28.4% vs +25.9% over the trailing year and carries a near-zero 0.07 correlation to the SPY. In a regime where CPI re-accelerated to 3.8% with a new Fed Chair inheriting an impossible mandate, and where the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed, gold provides a three-way hedge: inflation, geopolitical tail risk, and USD debasement risk.
picks.catalyst: May CPI print (June 10, 8:30 AM ET) — a hot print above 4.2% would reignite the inflation hedge bid for gold; BOJ hike June 16 would strengthen yen and potentially reduce carry trade selling pressure on gold
picks.entryNote: GLD at $397 is 22% below its 52-week high of $509.70 — a significant pullback in an asset with intact structural tailwinds; beta of 0.51 means half the downside of equities in a risk-off move
picks.exitTrigger: Fed achieves credible 2% CPI trajectory with two consecutive sub-2.5% core CPI prints AND Iran war fully resolves — removing both inflation and geopolitical premium simultaneously
picks.keyRisk: A risk-on melt-up on AI optimism combined with a dovish Fed surprise could strengthen the dollar and pressure gold further in the near term

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides quality-screened, dividend-growth equity exposure — a portfolio anchor that generates income while tilting toward Financials, Industrials, Consumer Staples, and Energy, all sectors currently ranked favorably in the sector rotation scorecard. In a stagflationary regime with negative real wage growth straining consumers, SCHD's dividend-growth methodology selects companies with strong FCF, low leverage, and pricing power — exactly the quality factor tilts that outperform in late-cycle environments. Appeared in every prior portfolio day at high conviction.
picks.catalyst: May CPI print (June 10) — a hot print accelerates rotation into value/dividend names from growth, directly benefiting SCHD's quality-value tilt
picks.entryNote: SCHD up modestly on the day (+0.28%), exhibiting defensive relative strength in a down-tech tape; dividend yield ~3.5% provides a return floor; quality-value factor historically outperforms in late-cycle inflationary regimes
picks.exitTrigger: Recession scenario materializes with earnings contracting >15% across the dividend-payer universe, eliminating dividend coverage ratios
picks.keyRisk: In a deep recession, dividend cuts across the financial and industrial sleeves of SCHD would reduce yield support and trigger NAV decline

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire under Greg Abel holds $397.4B in cash (up 6.5% from December 31) and restarted buybacks with $235M in Q1 repurchases — a cash-rich, all-weather compounder that actively benefits from higher rates through float reinvestment. Its Q1 2026 portfolio reshuffling (tripling GOOGL, initiating Delta, exiting Visa/MA/UNH) shows disciplined capital redeployment. BRK.B's diversified earnings base (insurance, BNSF rail, energy, consumer staples) means it has near-zero correlation to any single sector factor. At ~1.5x book, it offers intrinsic value support with a $397B cash optionality reserve that itself earns 4%+ risk-free.
picks.catalyst: Berkshire Annual Meeting follow-up disclosures and any Q2 2026 material acquisition announcement from the $397B war chest — Abel has signaled appetite for large-scale M&A
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~1.5x book — modest premium to intrinsic value vs. S&P at 4x+ book; BRK.B's $397B cash earns ~$16B/year at current T-bill rates, providing a meaningful earnings floor independent of equity market performance
picks.exitTrigger: Major acquisition at a price that significantly destroys book value per share, or insurance reserve deficiency materially above $5B emerges unexpectedly
picks.keyRisk: Greg Abel is unproven as CEO — portfolio reshuffling away from Buffett's legacy holdings introduces execution risk in capital allocation decisions

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF is the portfolio's explicit recession/deflation tail hedge — if the labor market weakens sharply from current 4.3% unemployment and AI capex pulls back, a recessionary dynamic would rapidly reprice front-end rates lower. JP Morgan assigns ~35% probability to a recession scenario, and bonds are identified as an effective hedge against both a recession and a reversal of AI optimism. The 10-year yield at 4.55% provides real coupon income while any growth disappointment would deliver capital gains. IEF captures this rate-cut optionality without the excessive duration risk of TLT.
picks.catalyst: A weak May CPI print (June 10) or any deterioration in labor market data (initial claims above 260K) would trigger immediate IEF rally as rate-cut probability reprices higher
picks.catalystWindow: June 10 CPI print is the immediate binary catalyst
picks.entryNote: 10-year at 4.55% — highest since early 2024; IEF at an attractive carry yield while providing convexity upside in a growth surprise to the downside; 7-10yr duration offers better risk/reward than ultra-long TLT given term premium uncertainty
picks.exitTrigger: CPI re-accelerates to 5%+ and Fed hikes twice in 2026, pushing 10-year yields above 5.5% — IEF would face mark-to-market losses exceeding coupon income
picks.keyRisk: Fiscal dominance — continued Treasury supply ($1.8T+ deficits) keeps term premium elevated and prevents the 10-year from rallying even in a growth slowdown

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is the quintessential late-cycle defensive anchor: Berkshire Hathaway holds KO as its third-largest position at 11.56% of portfolio ($30B+), providing a powerful smart-money endorsement. In a stagflationary regime with negative real wage growth, KO's brand pricing power, inelastic demand, 3%+ dividend yield, and global revenue diversification across 200+ markets make it one of the few names that outperforms in both inflationary and recessionary environments. Consumer Staples is moving from 'Improving' toward leadership per the June 9 sector update, signaling defensive rotation is beginning. KO has appeared in every prior portfolio.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — organic revenue growth update with pricing vs. volume decomposition; if pricing power holds above 4% while volumes recover, forward estimates revise upward
picks.entryNote: KO at ~22x forward P/E — slightly above 10-year median but justified by best-in-class dividend growth, brand moat, and defensive re-rating in a stagflationary regime; 3.2% yield provides income floor
picks.exitTrigger: Organic revenue growth falls below 3% for two consecutive quarters on volume deterioration (consumer trading down to private label), or a major currency devaluation in EM markets where KO derives 50%+ of revenue
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1 drug adoption (Ozempic/Mounjaro reducing caloric consumption) structurally compresses sugary beverage volumes over 3-5 years — a secular headwind not yet fully reflected in street estimates

summary: The portfolio is constructed for a late-cycle, stagflationary regime — the dominant macro call — where inflation re-acceleration (CPI 3.8% heading toward 4.18% per Cleveland Fed Nowcast), a barely-positive real policy rate (+45bps), and a geopolitically-driven energy shock define the operating environment ahead of the pivotal May CPI print on June 10. The 10-position growth book concentrates in three structural secular winners that are macro-resistant (NVDA/AVGO/ORCL in AI infrastructure, GOOGL/AMZN in AI-monetizing platforms), adds Energy (XOM) as the cheapest sector with the broadest positive earnings revision breadth, Defense/Industrials (RTX) as a geopolitical beneficiary, Capital Markets (GS/SPGI) as yield-curve steepening plays, and Healthcare (MRK) as a quality value anchor — spanning 5 distinct sectors. The defensive sleeve pairs GLD (inflation/geopolitical hedge) with SCHD (quality-income), BRK.B (all-weather cash fortress), IEF (rate-cut optionality), and KO (consumer staples compounder endorsed by Berkshire's 11.56% position). Nine of 10 growth book names carry over from prior days, with no thesis breaks — the only notable intra-day signal is the AI/tech sector pullback on the hot jobs print, which we treat as an accumulation opportunity rather than a thesis reversal. The dominant underwritten risk is a May CPI print above 4.2% driving rate-hike probability above 65% and triggering multiple compression across the long-duration growth names.
diversificationNote: The 10-name growth book spans five GICS sectors: Information Technology (NVDA, AVGO, ORCL — three names, the permitted maximum), Communication Services (GOOGL), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials (RTX), Energy (XOM), and Health Care (MRK); the two AI infrastructure semis (NVDA/AVGO) share secular exposure but occupy distinct value-chain positions (GPU compute vs. custom ASIC/networking), while ORCL competes at the cloud/software layer. The defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: gold commodity hedge (GLD), broad dividend-quality equity income (SCHD), all-weather equity compounder with cash optionality (BRK.B), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and consumer staples/inflation-resistant income (KO) — no exposure is duplicated.

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation

The AVGO miss was company-specific to custom ASIC guidance — NVDA's Blackwell/Rubin roadmap is intact, with Jensen Huang indicating up to $1 trillion in purchase orders through 2027. picks.entryNote: Down ~4% today from recent highs; still richly valued at ~35x forward earnings but supported by 100%+ YoY EPS growth. Today's dip toward 50dma creates better risk/reward than last week's highs.

picks.name: Oracle Corporation

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.

phaseData.macro:
Here is your senior macro strategy briefing for **Friday, June 5, 2026**:

---

## 📊 MACRO REGIME DASHBOARD — US EQUITIES | June 5, 2026

---

### THE DATA TAPE

**FED POLICY**

The Federal Reserve maintained the federal funds rate at 3.50%–3.75%
, now 
the third consecutive meeting where the committee chose to stand pat – following three consecutive cuts last year.
 The April 29 meeting was unusually contentious: 
the FOMC split along 8–4 lines, with officials expressing different reasons for their vote
 — 
the last time four members dissented was October 1992.
 On the dot plot, 
the median of the March 2026 Fed dot plot projections sees the fed funds rate falling just 25 bps in 2026 and 25 bps in 2027,
 implying a terminal rate around 3.25%. 
Notably, the "longer run" anchor has shifted upward to 3.0%, signaling a collective belief that the neutral rate is higher than previously estimated.
 
Fed officials foresee the funds rate settling down to its expected neutral level around 3.1%.
 Market-implied path: 
markets are pricing in no changes the rest of this year and well into 2027,
 with 
an ~85% chance of a quarter-point rate *hike* by year-end, up from 60% a week ago,
 as energy-driven inflation reaccelerates. The real policy rate (Fed funds midpoint 3.625% vs. core PCE ~3.3%) is barely positive — policy is only modestly restrictive relative to neutral at ~3.0–3.1%.

**YIELD CURVE**

As of June 4, 2026, the US Treasury yield curve is upward-sloping: the 2-Year yields 4.05%, the 10-Year 4.48%, and the 30-Year 4.98%.
 
The 10Y vs. 2Y spread (2s10s) is +42 bps; the 10Y vs. 3M spread (3m10y) is +76 bps.
 Crucially, 
the 10-year yield hovered around 4.48% after climbing on stronger-than-expected labor market data, reinforcing expectations of tighter Fed policy.
 
Elevated term premium and steady Treasury issuance continue to support higher long-maturity yields, consistent with upward pressure on long-end benchmarks.
 Term premium is rising — structurally driven by persistent inflation uncertainty and heavy sovereign supply.

**INFLATION — RE-ACCELERATING**

The annual inflation rate accelerated to 3.8% in April 2026, the highest since May 2023, compared to 3.3% in March — above forecasts of 3.7% — as the oil shock triggered by the war with Iran continues to push prices higher.
 
Energy costs jumped 17.9%, the steepest annual increase since September 2022, with gasoline up 28.4% and fuel oil up 54.3%.
 
Core CPI edged higher to 2.8% year-on-year, the highest since September, from 2.6% in March.
 On PCE: 
the PCE price index rose 0.4% in April (3.8% y/y), marking the third consecutive month of rapid inflation; the core PCE rose 0.2% (3.3% y/y).
 The March SEP projected 
a 30-bps increase in yearend 2026 PCE inflation (now 2.7%).
 
The softer-than-expected 0.24% m/m gain in core PCE in April buys the Fed a bit of time as it waits to see how higher oil prices will feed through to broader inflation pressures.
 Trajectory: **re-accelerating**, energy-led with secondary shelter stickiness.

**GROWTH**

Per the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow model, real GDP growth is currently tracking at 3% (quarter-over-quarter annualized basis) in Q2.
 However, 
Q1 GDP growth was revised down to 1.6% SAAR from 2.0% in the advance estimate, with the downward revision almost entirely due to a larger inventory drawdown.
 On labor: 
NFP is expected to rise ~85K in May (today's release), slowing from the 115K gain in April; the unemployment rate is forecast to hold at 4.3%.
 The three-month payroll run-rate is modest (~125–160K/month based on March's +178K and April's +115K). 
The third consecutive consensus-beating gain in nonfarm payrolls in May, if confirmed, should further reduce FOMC concern about downside labor market risks.
 On ISMs: 
Manufacturing PMI registered 54% in May, its highest reading since May 2022.
 
Among surveyed manufacturers, the Iran war was mentioned in 42% of comments and tariffs in 18%; 57% cited pricing volatility as an issue.
 Services: 
ISM Services PMI increased to 54.5 in May from 53.6 in April, the strongest gain in three months.
 
Employment contracted for a third consecutive month (47.9), while price pressures intensified to the highest since August 2022 (71.3) — diesel, gasoline, and oil again the primary culprits.


**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**

As of early 2026, broad investment-grade OAS is roughly ~80 bps.
 HY OAS data from FRED is current but specific levels were unavailable in today's pull — directionally, 
the HY-IG spread is trending down and SPX is rising, indicative of near-term risk-on sentiment.
 DXY: 
the US Dollar Index (DXY) fell toward 99.18 before recovering to ~99.45 on Thursday, as investors digested Fed commentary ahead of today's NFP release.
 Financial conditions are loose overall — the curve is positively sloped, spreads are tight, and dollar weakness has provided an additional easing impulse. 
Treasury yields have found support from escalating Middle East tensions, which keep oil prices elevated and add to inflationary pressures.


**EARNINGS BACKDROP**

The S&P 500 climbed to another record high recently, closing at 7,580.
 
The index extended its advance for a ninth consecutive week, rising more than 5% in May.
 
Technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February 2026,
 driven by AI infrastructure demand. 
The forward P/E on the S&P 500 stands at approximately 25.9x (Dec 2026 basis)
 — materially above the 10-year average of ~18–19x. 
The four largest hyperscalers — Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta — collectively spent north of $400 billion in capex in 2025, nearly 70% more than in 2024.
 The spread between AI capex intensity and near-term earnings delivery is a latent valuation risk.

**GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE**

A string of resilient US economic indicators has reinforced hawkish Fed expectations, while peace negotiations between the US and Iran are reportedly nearing their final stage — but Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said no meaningful progress had been made, and Iran-backed Hezbollah rejected a US-mediated ceasefire proposal, keeping geopolitical risks elevated.
 
Policymakers face an economic climate where inflation has held well above the Fed's 2% target, as tariffs and soaring energy prices complicate policy.
 
The next FOMC meeting is June 16–17, 2026.


---

## 🔴 PARAGRAPH 1 — REGIME CALL

**LATE-CYCLE.** The regime exhibits the classic late-cycle signature across all major pillars: 
positive but compressed spreads (2s10s +42 bps, 3m10y +76 bps) consistent with a cautious growth outlook and persistent inflation pressures that keep the Fed on hold.
 Nominal growth is still positive — GDPNow tracking +3% in Q2 — but Q1 was revised down to a soft 1.6%, labor is hiring at a decelerating pace, and 
services employment has now contracted for three consecutive months.
 Meanwhile, inflation is re-accelerating (CPI 3.8%, PCE 3.8% y/y) in a supply-shock context, leaving the Fed frozen on the sideline with a real policy rate barely above zero. This is textbook late-cycle: growth still above trend but losing momentum, inflation uncomfortably sticky/rising, policy rates on hold with a hawkish drift, term premium expanding, and equity markets priced for perfection at ~26x forward earnings.

---

## 🟡 PARAGRAPH 2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**What's Working:** Energy and defense are natural beneficiaries of the Iran war premium — 
S&P 500 forward EPS revisions are still trending higher even since the start of the Iran conflict, with energy estimates getting a boost.
 
Manufacturing at a 4-year high ISM (54%) with 19 straight months of broad expansion
 lifts Industrials and Materials. 
Technology estimates have been revised sharply higher,
 supported by the AI capex super-cycle; large-cap Tech/Semis (particularly AI infrastructure names like Oracle and Micron) are carrying the index to record highs. Quality/Profitability factors screen well in late cycle — companies with pricing power and low variable cost exposure to energy. Short-duration equities (Financials with steeper curve; Energy) and real assets outperform. **What's Breaking:** Rate-sensitive sectors are under pressure: 
inflation elevated, energy prices rising
 — this squeezes Consumer Discretionary margins and Housing. 
The prices-paid index at multi-year highs signals mounting supply-chain stress and margin pressure, likely restraining near-term investment and production for mid-cap Industrials.
 
Services employment is contracting for a third consecutive month
 — a leading stress indicator for labor-intensive sectors (Retail, Healthcare Services). Long-duration growth/SMID cap are most vulnerable to any further yield backup. The 26x forward P/E leaves essentially zero margin of safety for negative earnings surprises.

---

## 🟠 PARAGRAPH 3 — WHAT TO WATCH

**1. Today's May Nonfarm Payrolls (June 5) + June CPI (June 10):** 
Investors are awaiting today's nonfarm payrolls report for further clues on labor market strength
 — consensus expects ~85K but 
the third consecutive consensus-beating print
 would further close the door on any 2026 rate cut and 
markets are already pricing an 85% chance of a *hike* by year-end.
 June 10 CPI (May data) is equally pivotal: 
the Consumer Price Index for May 2026 is scheduled to be released on June 10 at 8:30 a.m.
 A third consecutive hot print would materially reprice the entire rates and equity complex. **2. June 16–17 FOMC Meeting + New Dot Plot:** 
The next FOMC meeting is June 16–17.
 With the April vote fracturing 8–4 and 
a majority of participants flagging that policy firming could become appropriate if inflation continues to run persistently above 2%,
 this meeting carries a live risk of a hawkish pivot in language or even a surprise hike — an event that would severely stress equities at current valuations. **3. Middle East Ceasefire/Escalation Binary:** 
Trump says peace talks are nearing their final stage, but Iran's foreign minister reports no meaningful progress, and Hezbollah has rejected a US-mediated Lebanon ceasefire,
 keeping the oil shock live. A genuine ceasefire would deliver a sharp disinflationary impulse — unlocking Fed cuts, compressing yields, and rotating the tape from Energy/Defense into rate-sensitive laggards. Escalation does the opposite and forces the Fed's hand higher. This is the single highest-optionality event on the 1–6 month horizon.

## 📊 US EQUITY MACRO BRIEFING — JUNE 8, 2026

### Key Data Dashboard

**FED POLICY**

**YIELD CURVE**

As of June 5, the Treasury curve is upward-sloping: **3-month = 3.72%**, **2-year = 4.17%**, **5-year = 4.29%**, **10-year = 4.55%**, **30-year = 5.01%**.
 
The **2s10s spread is +38 bps** and the **3m10y spread is +83 bps**.
 
These positive spreads reflect a cautious growth outlook with persistent inflation pressures, while elevated term premium and steady Treasury issuance continue to support higher long-maturity yields.
 
The broader shape — front end inverted relative to policy just months ago, belly flat, long end elevated — retains a classic late-cycle signature.


**INFLATION**

Headline CPI re-accelerated to **3.8% y/y** in April 2026 (the highest since May 2023, vs. 3.3% in March), coming in above the 3.7% forecast, as the oil shock from the US-Iran war continues to push prices higher.
 
Energy costs jumped **+17.9% y/y** — the steepest since September 2022 — driven by gasoline (+28.4%) and fuel oil (+54.3%).
 
Core CPI edged higher to **2.8% y/y**, the highest since September, with the monthly print at **+0.4%** — above February and March's 0.2% readings.
 
Headline **PCE came in at 3.77% y/y** (April, released May 28), with a 6.02% annualized 3-month pace.
 
The Cleveland Fed Nowcast projects May CPI will print **+0.46% m/m** (pushing the annual rate to ~4.18%), while core CPI looks more contained at +0.23% m/m / 2.82% y/y.
 Inflation is clearly **re-accelerating**, not decelerating — driven by a dual supply+demand shock (Iran war energy + AI capex cycle). 
Outside the energy complex, an increasingly dominant factor in the inflation surge is a basket of goods and services tied to the AI buildout — from computer equipment to software — with meaningful momentum as the capex cycle continues.


**GROWTH**

May nonfarm payrolls surged to **172,000** — roughly doubling the 85,000 consensus estimate — while unemployment held at **4.3%**.

Q2 personal consumption is tracking strong in the nowcast, but real wage growth is currently in negative territory and the savings rate is about half of what it was before the 2022 inflation spike, squeezing real income.
 
The composite ISM Prices Paid index eased slightly to 153.4 in May from 155.3 in April, but remains highly elevated.


**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**

Broad investment-grade (IG) OAS is roughly **~80 bps** as of early 2026.
 HY spreads have widened from recent tights on rising rate-hike fears but have not yet signaled a disorderly credit event.

Yields rose sharply and stocks slid Friday as rate-hike odds climbed on the hot payrolls print.

Goldman Sachs raised its S&P 500 year-end 2026 target to **8,000** (from 7,600), projecting 6% return, with **2026 EPS forecast of $340** (representing ~24% annual growth).

**GEOPOLITICAL TAPE**

Affordability concerns are unlikely to fade if the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed throughout the summer, mostly because of the likely persistence of higher energy prices.

### ¶1 — REGIME CALL

**Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Overhang.** The US economy is firmly in a **late-cycle, stagflationary regime**: 
the curve has moved from its 2022–2024 inversion toward a narrow positive slope, but the 3m10y has flipped negative again recently — a classic late-cycle signature —
 while 
inflation continues to outstrip nominal wage gains, squeezing real incomes and underscoring the stickiness of service and energy costs.
 The Fed is effectively trapped: 
AI demand shock and Iran war supply shock are occurring simultaneously, making the Fed's path increasingly difficult to navigate.


---

### ¶2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** 
Semiconductor companies are the primary direct beneficiaries of the AI capex cycle, with tech hardware, industrials, and utilities companies also receiving a large earnings boost from the AI buildout.
 
The S&P 500 has been on pace for 10 consecutive weeks of gains, with 8 of 11 sectors rising Thursday — the kind of breadth that reinforces faith in the rally — with financials and healthcare attracting buyers on rotation out of tech.
 Energy and commodity-linked equities are working structurally given the commodity supercycle impulse. **Breaking:** 
Real inflation-adjusted wage growth is in negative territory, and today's consumer — especially at the lower end of the K-shaped economy — is in a weaker spot.
 Rate-sensitive sectors (REITs, small-cap growth, consumer discretionary ex-luxury) face headwinds as 
yields rose sharply on rate-hike odds climbing post-payrolls.
 
Only about 56% of S&P 500 stocks trade above their 50-day moving average despite the index trading near record highs
 — a warning sign of extreme top-heaviness and fragile breadth beneath the surface.

1. **May CPI (June 10, 8:30 AM ET):** 
This is the single most market-moving release of the week, with the Cleveland Fed Nowcast flagging upside risk to a 4.18% y/y headline print.
 A print above 4.2% would likely push rate-hike odds above 65% and re-price the front end sharply higher.

2. **Bank of Japan Rate Decision (June 16):** 
Japan's PPI has already accelerated to 4.9% y/y in April and is expected to push above 5.0% in May, with markets pricing 82% odds of a BOJ hike — a hot print would tip those odds close to certainty.
 A BOJ hike would trigger yen carry-trade unwinding, a potential cross-asset shock for US equities and Treasuries.

3. **Strait of Hormuz / Iran War Escalation:** 
Inflation has jumped to the top of concern, chiefly because of the rapid increase in energy's contribution to both CPI and PCE — a driver that was entirely absent from pre-2026 forecasts.
 Any further escalation that tightens oil supply materially would force the Fed's hand and compress equity multiples in a classic stagflationary derating.

PLTR has been a standout beneficiary of the AI boom, but the stock has **recently lost momentum and is down 23% year-to-date**.
 Already halfway to the threshold — further re-rating risk is significant.

### 🔴 2. NVDA — NVIDIA Corporation
**Data Center Concentration + Multiple Compression Risk**


As of June 1, 2026, NVIDIA has a **price-to-sales ratio of 21.56** — 235.3% higher than the industry median at 6.43. Its price-earnings ratio is 34.4x and EV/EBITDA is 23.5x.
 
Price-to-free-cash-flow is 46.2x. AAII assigns NVDA a **Value Score of 11** — rated "Ultra Expensive."


The key single-stock risk is **hyperscaler customer concentration**: 
NVIDIA's Data Center segment currently generates approximately **90% of its total revenue**. If hyperscaler customers are pressured to cut costs or pivot toward cheaper, self-designed custom silicon, Nvidia stock may lose its main growth driver.
 
If the stock appears overvalued, the primary risk is **multiple compression** — if Nvidia fails to meet quarterly earnings beats, investors reset growth expectations, and the P/E could contract toward the lower end of the industry range, potentially leading to a sharp downward price adjustment.

### 🔴 3. TSLA — Tesla, Inc.
**Autonomy Premium with Negative FCF + Execution Slippage**


The market is repricing Tesla from "car + batteries" to "autonomy platform." However, the robotaxi timeline has softened (cities relabeled "preparations underway," unsupervised "ramping" only in Dallas/Houston), while **capex jumps to $25B+ and Tesla guides negative free cash flow through 2026**.
 
Muted post-earnings reaction plus higher capex and negative FCF guidance sets up a **"multiple compression" risk if autonomy timelines keep slipping**.


The bull case requires flawless Cybercab execution and FSD expansion — both are high-variance. 
The stock has recovered from drawdowns of 40%, 50%, even 70% multiple times
 — but each recovery required a *new* catalyst. With the current autonomy timeline slipping and no near-term FCF, the downside to current valuation remains severe.

### 🔴 4. SNOW — Snowflake
**Consumption Model Volatility + Intense Competitive Pressure**


After a rough 2024, Snowflake's product revenue reaccelerated to **29% growth**, and its Cortex AI layer positions it as a data backbone for enterprise AI.
 However, the risk profile is asymmetric: Snowflake's consumption-based model creates **revenue visibility risk** in any macro deceleration (enterprises throttle consumption before canceling SaaS seats). Competitive pressure from Databricks, Google BigQuery, and AWS is intensifying. At ~15x forward revenue, any growth deceleration from 29% toward 20% triggers an automatic multiple reset. Customer concentration (top 10 customers historically >25% of revenue) compounds idiosyncratic risk.

### 🔴 5. CELH — Celsius Holdings
**Deteriorating Gross Margins + Pepsi Channel Dependency**

CELH is the most fragile name in the universe on a risk/reward basis. The company is almost entirely dependent on PepsiCo as its U.S. distribution channel (~60–65% of revenues). Margin compression from over-distribution costs and increased promotional spend hit gross margins materially in 2024–2025. If Pepsi renegotiates distribution terms or pivots promotional dollars to competing energy brands, CELH faces a double hit: top-line miss + margin collapse. Valuation at 30–40x forward earnings still prices in recovery to 20%+ revenue growth — highly vulnerable to a guidance cut.

### ⚠️ TAIL RISK #1 — Stagflation Re-Acceleration / Fed Forced Hike
**Probability: ELEVATED (35–45%)**

**What triggers it:**

The DXY is near 99 on sticky U.S. inflation (CPI 3.8%), and **cuts are off the table for now — markets even price a possible December rate hike**.
 
The Fed navigated pandemic-related crises but its 2% inflation target has proved elusive, and inflation still runs at 3.1%+.
 
The conflict: a dovish-leaning new Fed Chair has inherited 3.8% inflation and **cannot simply cut into that**.
 If Kevin Warsh's first FOMC (June 16–17) signals hawkish pivot, or another oil spike re-accelerates CPI toward 4.5%, the Fed hikes 25–50bp into a slowing economy.

**What breaks:**
- Long-duration tech (NVDA, PLTR, SNOW, ARM, MRVL) — rate-sensitive multiples compress violently
- TLT/IEF — price declines as yields spike
- REITs (AMT, PLD, EQIX, O) — cap rate repricing
- 
Sticky inflation could force the Fed to tighten liquidity, putting pressure on **risky assets especially those that benefited from easy monetary policy**.

### 📊 VIX / Volatility Regime


As of June 9, the **VIX is at 20.45**, up 8.09% on the day. At market close June 8, the VIX was at 18.92, with **realized historical volatility over the past 30 days of only 13.51**.
 This is a critical divergence: **implied vol (VIX ~20) is running 50% above realized vol (~13.5)** — the options market is paying a premium for protection that realized price action doesn't yet justify. Either vol sellers are being punished prematurely, or the market *knows* something is coming.


After cresting at 29.49 in early March amid rising Middle East tensions and oil price shocks, the VIX retreated into the mid-teens through April and May. Although below first-quarter peaks, **volatility remains elevated compared with the unusually calm readings seen in late 2025**.


**VIX Term Structure:** 
VIX is attempting to build a base above the key 17 support zone after a prolonged downtrend. The recent impulsive candle suggests **volatility buyers are becoming active**, while price is testing the lower edge of the 21–24 gap area.
 This points to **mild backwardation / flattening term structure** — a warning signal that near-term fear is rising relative to longer-dated expectations.
